prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. He's hoping to get into a competitive writing program with something like this. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

Let me carefully evaluate this essay against the prompt and typical standards for competitive writing programs.

**The prompt:** Write about a time you failed and what you learned (~500 words)

**Word count:** Let me estimate... this appears to be roughly 420-450 words, so slightly under the target but not egregiously so.

**What works well:**

1. **Clear narrative arc**: The story has a beginning (deciding to try out), middle (the tryouts/failure), and resolution (joining debate, reflection). It's easy to follow.

2. **Specific, concrete details**: The essay includes specific moments - Marcus beating him one-on-one, not knowing what a pick-and-roll was, the kid telling him to "stay out of the way," the Friday list going up. These details make it feel real.

3. **Authentic voice**: The writing sounds like a real person, not overly polished. Phrases like "Obviously" and "sounds like a weird pivot" feel genuine.

4. **Honest vulnerability**: The author admits he lied to his mom, acknowledges the sting of Marcus making the team, and doesn't try to pretend the failure didn't hurt.

5. **Genuine learning**: The lessons aren't preachy or obvious. They feel earned and reflected on. The three levels of learning (casual vs. competitive, failure leading elsewhere, not knowing what you don't know) build nicely.

6. **Personal growth narrative**: The pivot to debate isn't just a "silver lining" - it becomes the more important story and shows actual growth.

**What could be stronger:**

1. **Show vs. tell in parts**: Some reflections could be embedded in scene better. For instance, "I didn't know what a pick and roll was" is telling - showing the confusion in the moment might be more powerful.

2. **Slight clichés**: Phrases like "failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information" feel a bit too polished/canned for the otherwise authentic voice. The ending reflection is a bit generic.


3. **Sensory gaps**: The gym scene lacks texture—what did it smell like, how did his hands feel during the scrimmage? Adding these details would deepen the experience.

4. **Underdeveloped pivot**: The debate turn is actually the essay's strongest moment but gets glossed over. Showing his first debate experience or what clicked about it would strengthen the whole arc considerably.

5. **Tonal shift**: The final paragraph becomes more reflective and abstract, pulling away from the personal, grounded voice that carries the rest of the piece.

6. **Room to expand**: At under 500 words, there's space to develop the debate experience and early realizations more fully, which would also help the essay feel more complete.

**For a competitive writing program:**

This is solid work. It's not going to blow people away with originality—basketball tryout failure is a common topic—but it's well-executed, honest, and shows genuine reflection. The voice is strong and the pivot to debate is interesting. However, it lacks the distinctive voice, surprising insights, or literary craft that would make it stand out in a highly competitive pool.

I'd rate this a 7/10. Technical execution is solid, the voice feels authentic and strong, but originality is moderate and the insights, while good, don't quite reach exceptional. The overall effectiveness is there, but it needs more distinctive elements to compete at the highest level.

# Grade: 7/10

## What Works Really Well

**Authentic voice and honesty** — This essay sounds like a real person, not someone performing. The casual language ("Obviously," "sounds like a weird pivot") combined with genuine vulnerability makes it engaging. Your friend isn't hiding behind false confidence or trying too hard.

**Strong specific details** — The pick-and-roll, Marcus making the team, the kid saying "stay out of the way," the Friday list—these concrete moments make the failure feel real instead of abstract.

**Interesting narrative turn** — The pivot to debate is the essay's most distinctive element. It transforms this from a generic "I failed and learned a lesson" story into something more interesting: "Failure redirected me to something better." This is genuinely worth exploring more.

**Layered learning** — The essay progresses from surface lessons (casual ≠ competitive) to deeper ones (you don't know what you don't know). That progression feels earned rather than preached.

## What Could Strengthen It

**The ending gets generic** — "Failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information" sounds like fortune-cookie wisdom and doesn't quite match the authentic voice of the rest. The reflection is good, but it could be more specific to *this* experience.

**Underdeveloped debate section** — This is ironically the most interesting part but it's glossed over. What was the first debate like? A small scene here would show growth rather than just tell us about it.

**Could use more sensory/scene details** — The scrimmage scene is told rather than shown. What did that moment of being told to "stay out of the way" actually *feel* like?

**Slightly under length** — At ~420 words vs. 500, there's room to expand the debate pivot and scrimmage scene.

## Bottom Line

This is solid, honest work that would be competitive for many programs, but in a *highly* selective pool, it lacks the distinctive voice or surprising insight that makes essays memorable. It's a good "B+" essay that could become an "A" with revisions focusing on the debate pivot and less generic reflection.

# Grade: 7/10

## What Works

**Strong narrative structure**: Clear arc from setup through failure to reflection. Your friend tells a complete story efficiently.

**Authentic voice**: This reads like a genuine teen perspective—conversational but intelligent. Lines like "Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things" feel earned, not forced.

**Specific details**: The pick-and-roll reference, the scrimmage teammate saying "stay out of the way," Marcus making the team—these concrete moments make it real rather than generic.

**Good reflection**: The insight about "you don't know what you don't know" shows genuine thinking about the experience, not just surface-level takeaways.

**Underdeveloped emotional depth**: The immediate aftermath of rejection could hit harder. What did the list moment actually *feel* like?

**Minor prose issues**: A few clichés ("it worked out fine") and some sentences are functional rather than elegant.

## Bottom Line

This is **competent, honest, and well-structured**—but for a competitive program, it reads like good work rather than standout work. It demonstrates writing ability, but not the originality or depth that typically gets noticed. 

**Specific suggestion**: Spend more time on either the emotional weight of the failure *or* the unexpected discovery of debate. Go deeper into one element rather than summarizing the whole arc.

prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. He's hoping to get into a competitive writing program with something like this. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. He's hoping to get into a competitive writing program with something like this. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

## What Works

**Strengths:**
- **Clear narrative arc** – The story has a genuine beginning, middle, and resolution. It's easy to follow and feels honest.
- **Specific details** – "Pick and roll," the scrimmage scene, the kid telling you to stay out of the way. These concrete moments ground the essay and make it vivid.

## What Needs Work

- **Tell vs. Show** – You *tell* us you realized you were out of your league, but you don't *show* us that moment. What specifically happened that made it click? What did it feel like in your body?

## The Bottom Line

This is solid, honest work that shows self-awareness. But for a competitive program, it needs more depth, richer writing, or a more surprising emotional insight.
